points.text: Implement rate limiting on login attempts per IP to curb brute‑force traffic.
Add exponential backoff and retry logic for DB operations to reduce lock contention.
Introduce a short account lockout after several failed attempts to deter attacks.
Optimize login‑related queries and use connection pooling to lower DB latency.
Consider read replicas or sharding for the authentication tables to distribute load.
Move read‑heavy login queries to read replicas or use row‑level/optimistic locking to shrink transaction windows and cut lock contention.
Introduce per‑IP rate limiting or progressive back‑off on login attempts to curb brute‑force traffic and reduce load spikes.
Wrap the authentication service in a circuit‑breaker with a short timeout to shed excess load during peak traffic.
Shorten login transaction scope; use read‑only transactions with READ COMMITTED to reduce lock hold time.
Create indexes on username/email (and any login‑lookup columns) to avoid full table scans under load.
Separate audit of failed attempts into its own table or async queue to keep user table locks minimal.
Use SELECT … FOR SHARE or SKIP LOCKED for concurrent login reads to prevent blocking on the same rows.
Apply optimistic concurrency (e.g., version column) for login‑counter updates instead of exclusive row locks.
Add rate limiting or IP‑based throttling on the login endpoint to curb brute‑force attempts and reduce request load.
Implement exponential backoff or temporary account lockout after repeated failed attempts to deter credential stuffing.
Offload credential checks to a read‑only replica or cache (e.g., Redis) to alleviate DB lock contention during peaks.
Issue short‑lived JWTs after successful authentication so subsequent requests avoid DB lookups for session state.
Introduce CAPTCHA or bot‑challenge after a few failed logins to block automated attacks while preserving user experience.
Deploy a per‑IP/per‑account rate limiter on the login endpoint to cap attempts during spikes.
Add credential‑stuffing detection (IP reputation, device fingerprint) and trigger step‑up challenges for suspicious traffic.
Introduce exponential back‑off or temporary lockout after N failures to reduce DB writes and contention.
Offload password hash verification to a fast cache or read‑replica, or process it asynchronously to lessen DB lock contention.
